does: This paper adapts visual search paradigms from cognitive psychology to probe the visual processing mechanisms of multimodal large language models. The authors conduct controlled experiments using images with varying colours, sizes, and lighting to test whether MLLMs exhibit the pop-out effect—where humans detect salient features independently of distractor set size. They find that advanced MLLMs show human-like pop-out effects for single-feature searches involving colour or size, demonstrate capacity limits for multi-feature conjunctive searches, and exhibit biases consistent with natural scene priors like lighting direction assumptions. The work combines behavioural testing with targeted fine-tuning and mechanistic interpretability analyses to characterise these effects, demonstrating that visual search tasks provide quantitative, interpretable diagnostics of model visual processing beyond standard accuracy metrics.
connects: This work builds on decades of cognitive psychology research on visual attention and feature integration theory, particularly Treisman and Gelade's framework distinguishing pre-attentive pop-out from effortful serial search. It extends recent efforts to evaluate MLLMs beyond task accuracy, contributing to a growing literature questioning whether these models truly understand visual scenes or exploit superficial statistical patterns. The paper connects to mechanistic interpretability research that dissects neural network representations, and to evaluation frameworks that apply human-like cognitive tasks to AI systems. It responds to concerns about the opacity of black-box model evaluations and builds on earlier work comparing human and machine vision, while leveraging controlled experimental design principles established in human perception research to yield more interpretable insights into model capabilities and limitations.
enables: This framework enables future work to develop fine-grained diagnostics for visual understanding in MLLMs, moving beyond coarse accuracy metrics toward mechanistic understanding of how models process visual features. The approach could be extended to probe other cognitive phenomena—motion perception, gestalt principles, depth cues—establishing a comprehensive battery of psychologically-grounded tests. The findings suggest pathways for improving model robustness and human-alignment by identifying and correcting problematic visual priors or search strategies. The methodology itself creates opportunities for comparative studies across model architectures and training regimes, and could inform design of more interpretable vision components in future systems. Additionally, these diagnostic tools could help identify failure modes in safety-critical applications where understanding model visual reasoning is essential, and support development of models with more human-like visual scene understanding.
